sections.heading: Schedule 1
sections.title: Schedule 1 commencement timing
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date for Schedule 1 of the Act. It states that Schedule 1 commences immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences, with a fallback date of 1 November 2010 if section 3 does not commence. The provision is conditional on the commencement of section 3.

requirements.text: Schedule 1 commences immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences
If section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, Schedule 1 does not commence at all

sections.source_quote: 2. Schedule 1 Immediately after the commencement of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. However, if section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provision(s) do not commence at all.

sections.heading: Schedule 2
sections.title: Schedule 2 commencement timing
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date for Schedule 2 of the Act. It states that Schedule 2 commences the day after the end of a 6-month period beginning from the commencement date of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010, with a fallback date of 1 May 2011 if section 3 does not commence. The provision is conditional on the commencement of section 3.

requirements.text: Schedule 2 commences the day after the end of the period of 6 months beginning on the day section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences
If section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, Schedule 2 does not commence at all

sections.source_quote: 3. Schedule 2 The day after the end of the period of 6 months beginning on the day section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences. However, if section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provision(s) do not commence at all.

sections.heading: Schedule 3,
sections.title: Schedule 3 items 1-14 commencement timing
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date for items 1 to 14 of Schedule 3. It states that these items commence immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences, with a fallback date of 1 November 2010 if section 3 does not commence. The provision is conditional on the commencement of section 3.

requirements.text: Items 1 to 14 of Schedule 3 commence immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences
If section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, items 1 to 14 of Schedule 3 do not commence at all

sections.source_quote: 4. Schedule 3, items 1 to 14 Immediately after the commencement of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. However, if section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provision(s) do not commence at all.

sections.heading: Schedule 3,
sections.title: Schedule 3 item 15 commencement timing
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date for item 15 of Schedule 3. It states that this item commences the day after the end of a 6-month period beginning from the commencement date of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010, with a fallback date of 1 May 2011 if section 3 does not commence. The provision is conditional on the commencement of section 3.

requirements.text: Item 15 of Schedule 3 commences the day after the end of the period of 6 months beginning on the day section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences
If section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, item 15 of Schedule 3 does not commence at all

sections.source_quote: 5. Schedule 3, item 15 The day after the end of the period of 6 months beginning on the day section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences. However, if section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provision(s) do not commence at all.

sections.heading: Schedule 3,
sections.title: Schedule 3 items 16-40 commencement timing
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date for items 16 to 40 of Schedule 3. It states that these items commence immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences, with a fallback date of 1 November 2010 if section 3 does not commence. The provision is conditional on the commencement of section 3.

requirements.text: Items 16 to 40 of Schedule 3 commence immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences
If section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, items 16 to 40 of Schedule 3 do not commence at all

sections.source_quote: 6. Schedule 3, items 16 to 40 Immediately after the commencement of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. However, if section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provision(s) do not commence at all.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to
sections.title: Schedules 4-7 commencement timing
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date for Schedules 4 to 7. It states that these schedules commence immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences, with a fallback date of 1 November 2010 if section 3 does not commence. The provision is conditional on the commencement of section 3.

requirements.text: Schedules 4 to 7 commence immediately after section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 commences
If section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, Schedules 4 to 7 do not commence at all

sections.source_quote: 7. Schedules 4 to 7 Immediately after the commencement of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. However, if section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provision(s) do not commence at all.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Publication of information requirements for agencies
sections.summary: Agencies must publish required information to the public generally, with optional publication to specific groups. Information must be accessible via website downloads or links. Agencies may charge for access only if the person doesn't download directly and the charge covers specific reproduction costs. Charges must be published alongside the information. The Information Commissioner may assist agencies with identifying and preparing information for publication.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires agencies to publish information publicly via websites, with specific conditions on charges and access. Relevant for assessing how information is shared across borders when agencies publish data.

requirements.text: Agency must publish information required or permitted under this Part to members of the public generally
Agency must publish information to particular classes of persons or entities if appropriate
Agency must publish information on website by making available for download or providing links to download
Agency may impose charge for accessing information only if person does not download directly
Agency may impose charge only if charge covers specific reproduction costs
Agency must publish details of charge in same way as information is published
Information Commissioner may provide assistance to agencies in identifying and preparing information for publication
Information Commissioner may determine how information is published

sections.source_quote: An agency must publish information that is required or permitted to be published under this Part in accordance with this section. The agency must publish the information: (a) to members of the public generally; and (b) if the agency considers that it is appropriate to do so—to particular classes of persons or entities.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions for information access periods
sections.summary: This section amends the open access periods for records and Cabinet notebooks. It reduces the standard open access period from 25 to 15 years for most records, with specific calculations for different record creation years. Cabinet notebooks have different open access periods, with some requiring 31 years after creation for full access.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets specific timeframes for when government records become publicly accessible after creation, relevant for data retention and publication timelines in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Open access period for records: 15 years after creation for records from 2000 onwards
Open access period for records: 21 years after creation for records from 2001 onwards
Open access period for Cabinet notebooks: 31 years after creation for records from 1991 onwards

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Act, subject to sections 22A and 22B, work out when a record is in the open access period in accordance with the following table: Open access period for records Item If the record came into existence in any of the following years (ending on 31 December): the record is in the open access period on and after the following day: 13 a year (the creation year) after 2000 1 January in the year that is 21 years after the creation year.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions for information disclosure requests
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions under which agencies or Ministers may refuse access to documents. It specifies that exempt information may be withheld if disclosure would be unreasonable, impracticable, or if the document contains irrelevant information. Agencies must publish information on websites, provide edited copies where necessary, and charge only for specific reproduction costs. The section also sets a 10-day publication deadline and defines working days for compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for exempting information from disclosure, including when edited copies must be provided and when charges can be imposed. It impacts DTIAs by defining when information can be withheld and the requirements for publishing edited copies.

requirements.text: The agency or Minister must publish the information on a website by making it available for downloading
The agency or Minister must publish a link to another website where the information can be downloaded
The agency or Minister must publish other details of how the information may be obtained
The agency may impose a charge for accessing information only if the person does not directly download it from the website
The agency may impose a charge only if it reimburses specific reproduction costs or incidental costs
The agency or Minister must publish details of any charge in the same way as the information
The agency or Minister must comply with this section within 10 working days after the person is given access to the document

sections.source_quote: The agency, or the Minister, must publish the information to members of the public generally on a website by: (a) making the information available for downloading from the website; or (b) publishing on the website a link to another website, from which the information can be downloaded; or (c) publishing on the website other details of how the information may be obtained.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions for documents disclosing deliberative processes
sections.summary: This section specifies that documents involving deliberative processes (e.g., opinions, advice, recommendations) are conditionally exempt from disclosure unless disclosure would be contrary to the public interest. It excludes operational information and purely factual material. The exemption applies to Commonwealth and State agencies, with specific exceptions for certain types of reports and decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates conditions for exempting documents involving internal deliberations, which may impact data transfer assessments when handling sensitive internal communications across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: A document is conditionally exempt if its disclosure would disclose matter in the nature of, or relating to, opinion, advice or recommendation obtained, prepared or recorded, or consultation or deliberation
Deliberative matter does not include operational information
Deliberative matter does not include purely factual material
This section does not apply to reports of scientific or technical experts
This section does not apply to reports of bodies or organisations established within an agency
This section does not apply to the record of final decisions

sections.source_quote: A document is conditionally exempt if its disclosure under this Act would disclose matter (deliberative matter) in the nature of, or relating to, opinion, advice or recommendation obtained, prepared or recorded, or consultation or deliberation that has taken place, in the course of, or for the purposes of, the deliberative processes involved in the functions of an agency or Minister or of the Government of the Commonwealth.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions for documents concerning business and professional affairs
sections.summary: This section establishes that documents containing information about a person's business or professional affairs, or an organisation's business, commercial or financial affairs, may be conditionally exempt if disclosure would unreasonably affect that person or organisation. It excludes trade secrets and applies to Commonwealth and State agencies, with specific exceptions for certain types of requests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer assessments by defining when business-related information can be exempted, which may impact cross-border data sharing involving sensitive commercial information.

requirements.text: A document is conditionally exempt if its disclosure would unreasonably affect a person's business or professional affairs
A document is conditionally exempt if its disclosure would unreasonably affect an organisation's business, commercial or financial affairs
Subsection (1) does not apply to trade secrets or other information covered by section 47
Subsection (1) does not apply to requests by a person for information concerning their own business or professional affairs
Subsection (1) does not apply to requests by a person for information concerning an organisation they own or act for

sections.source_quote: A document is conditionally exempt if its disclosure under this Act would disclose information concerning a person in respect of his or her business or professional affairs or concerning the business, commercial or financial affairs of an organisation or undertaking, in a case in which the disclosure of the information: would, or could reasonably be expected to, unreasonably affect that person adversely in respect of his or her lawful business or professional affairs or that organisation or undertaking in respect of its lawful business, commercial or financial affairs

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions for Information Commissioner and Access Decisions
sections.summary: This section adds specific definitions to the Freedom of Information Act 1982, including terms like 'access grant decision', 'access refusal decision', 'affected third party', and 'internal review'. These definitions clarify the scope of decisions subject to internal review and the parties involved in the review process. The definitions apply to agencies and the Information Commissioner when handling access requests to documents.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key terms used in internal and external reviews of access decisions, which impacts how data transfer impact assessments must account for decision-making processes and third-party involvement in data access requests.

requirements.text: access grant decision has the meaning given by section 53B
access refusal decision has the meaning given by section 53A
affected third party has the meaning given by section 53C
internal review has the meaning given by sections 54 and 54A
IC review applicant has the meaning given by section 54J
IC review application has the meaning given by section 54H
implementation notice has the meaning given by section 89
Information Commissioner has the meaning given by the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010
internal review applicant has the meaning given by section 54B
investigation recommendations has the meaning given by section 88
investigation results has the meaning given by section 87
respondent agency has the meaning given by subsections 69(2) and 70(2)
review parties has the meaning given by section 55A
vexatious applicant declaration has the meaning given by section 89K

sections.source_quote: access grant decision has the meaning given by section 53B. access refusal decision has the meaning given by section 53A. affected third party has the meaning given by section 53C. internal review has the meaning given by sections 54 and 54A.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Information Commissioner review applications for access decisions
sections.summary: This section establishes procedures for applying to the Information Commissioner for review of access refusal or access grant decisions. Agencies or Ministers must make decisions within specified timeframes after notice of the decision. Review applications must include copies of notices and details for communication. The Information Commissioner may extend time for applications if reasonable, and must notify affected third parties if required. The review process includes preliminary inquiries, discretion to not review, and procedures for handling applications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines review procedures for access decisions that impact data transfer and disclosure processes. It specifies time limits for applications, requirements for notifying affected third parties, and conditions under which the Information Commissioner may extend time or decide not to review a case. These elements affect how data access decisions are challenged and reviewed across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: IC review application must be in writing and include details of how notices may be sent
IC review application must include a copy of the notice given under section 26 of the IC reviewable decision
IC review application may contain particulars of the basis for disputing the IC reviewable decision
Information Commissioner must provide assistance to persons making IC review applications
IC review application must be sent to the Information Commissioner via delivery, postage, or electronic communication
Agency or Minister must notify affected third parties as soon as practicable if an IC review application is made for an access refusal decision
Agency or Minister must give copy of notice to Information Commissioner
Information Commissioner may extend time for making IC review application if reasonable
Information Commissioner may require IC review applicant to notify specified persons affected by application
Person notified under subsection (4) may oppose application within time required by Information Commissioner
Information Commissioner must give reasonable opportunity to present cases if opposition is made

sections.source_quote: An IC review application must be in writing, and must: (a) give details of how notices under this Part may be sent to the IC review applicant; and (b) include a copy of the notice given under section 26 of the IC reviewable decision for which an IC review is sought.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: IC review procedures for hearings and participation
sections.summary: This section outlines procedures for conducting IC reviews, including conducting reviews with minimal formality, ensuring reasonable opportunity for review parties to present cases, and timely completion of reviews. The Information Commissioner must conduct reviews with minimal formality, provide review parties with reasonable opportunity to present their case, and complete reviews as promptly as possible given applicable laws and considerations. The Information Commissioner may also hold hearings publicly unless confidential matters prevent it, and may allow participation via alternative means such as telephone.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details procedural requirements for IC reviews that impact data transfer impact assessments by specifying how the Information Commissioner conducts reviews with review parties, including hearing procedures and participation methods.

requirements.text: The Information Commissioner must conduct IC reviews with minimal formality and technicality given applicable laws and considerations
The Information Commissioner must ensure each review party is given reasonable opportunity to present their case
The Information Commissioner must conduct IC reviews in as timely a manner as possible given applicable considerations
The Information Commissioner may hold hearings publicly unless satisfied that confidentiality prevents it
The Information Commissioner may allow review parties to participate in hearings via alternative communication methods
The Information Commissioner may give written directions on IC review procedures

sections.source_quote: The Information Commissioner must, in relation to an IC review: (a) conduct the IC review with as little formality and as little technicality as is possible given: (i) the requirements of this Act; (ii) the requirements of any other law; and (iii) a proper consideration of the matters before the Information Commissioner; (b) ensure that each review party is given a reasonable opportunity to present his or her case; and (c) conduct the IC review in as timely a manner as is possible given the matters mentioned in subparagraphs (a)(i) to (iii).

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: IC review procedures for legal questions and document handling
sections.summary: This section details procedures for IC reviews, including the ability to refer questions of law to the Federal Court of Australia, sending relevant documents to the Federal Court, and the Information Commissioner's authority to make decisions on IC reviews. The Information Commissioner may refer questions of law to the Federal Court, send documents to the court, and publish decisions on IC reviews to the public. The Information Commissioner must also ensure compliance with decisions made during IC reviews.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies procedures for IC reviews that impact data transfer impact assessments by detailing how the Information Commissioner handles legal questions, documents, and decisions during reviews.

requirements.text: The Information Commissioner may refer questions of law to the Federal Court of Australia during an IC review
The Information Commissioner must send all relevant documents to the Federal Court of Australia
The Information Commissioner must publish IC review decisions to the public

sections.source_quote: The Information Commissioner may, at any time during an IC review, refer a question of law arising in an IC review to the Federal Court of Australia for decision. The Information Commissioner must cause all documents and information in the possession of the Information Commissioner that relate to the IC review and to the reference to be sent to the Federal Court of Australia.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Information Commissioner's document handling powers during IC reviews
sections.summary: This section outlines the Information Commissioner's authority to handle documents during IC reviews, including taking possession, making copies, extracting information, and holding documents for review purposes. It also mandates that the Commissioner must permit inspection by entitled parties at reasonable times. The provisions establish specific conditions for document access and production, particularly for exempt documents under national security or cabinet categories.
sections.dtia_summary: These provisions define document handling procedures during IC reviews, impacting data transfer and storage by requiring specific access controls and document handling protocols for exempt documents, which may affect cross-border data flows involving sensitive national security information.

requirements.text: The Information Commissioner may take possession of documents produced in accordance with a notice
The Information Commissioner may make copies of documents
The Information Commissioner may take extracts from documents
The Information Commissioner may hold documents for a period necessary for IC review
The Information Commissioner must permit inspection by entitled parties for IC review
The Information Commissioner must permit inspection at any reasonable time

sections.source_quote: The Information Commissioner may do any of the following in relation to any documents produced in accordance with a notice under subsection 55R(3): (a) take possession of the documents; (b) make copies of the documents; (c) take extracts from the documents; (d) hold the documents for a period that is necessary for the purposes of the IC review.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Tribunal powers for document searches and record amendments
sections.summary: This section amends the Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975 by inserting new powers for the Tribunal to require further searches for documents when access is refused or purportedly granted. It also establishes conditions under which amendments to records of opinions can be made, including requirements that the opinion was based on a mistake of fact or the author was biased. The Tribunal must not amend records that are court decisions or involve matters already determined by internal review, the Information Commissioner, courts, or tribunals.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by establishing conditions under which the Tribunal may require further document searches and amend records of opinions, with specific restrictions on amending court-related records or decisions involving prior determinations.

requirements.text: Tribunal may require further searches for documents when access is refused or purportedly granted
Tribunal may make a decision requiring amendment to a record of an opinion if the opinion was based on a mistake of fact or the author was biased
Tribunal must not amend records that are court decisions
Tribunal must not amend records if the decision involves matters already determined by internal review, Information Commissioner, courts, or tribunals

sections.source_quote: 58AA (1) The Tribunal may, in a decision on an application to the Tribunal under section 57A, make a decision that requires, or that has the effect of requiring, that an amendment be made to a record that relates to a record of an opinion only if the Tribunal is satisfied of either (or both) of the following: (a) the opinion was based on a mistake of fact; (b) the author of the opinion was biased, unqualified to form the opinion or acted improperly in conducting the factual inquiries that led to the formation of the opinion. (2) The Tribunal must not, in a decision on an application under section 57A, make a decision that requires, or that has the effect of requiring, that an amendment be made to a record if it is satisfied of either of the following: (a) the record is a record of a decision, under an enactment, by a court, tribunal, authority or person; (b) the decision whether to amend the record involves the determination of a question that the person seeking amendment of the record is, or has been, entitled to have determined by the agency (on internal review), the Information Commissioner, a court or tribunal.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Tribunal procedures for third-party notifications and onus of proof
sections.summary: This section amends the Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975 by requiring agencies or Ministers to notify affected third parties when a review application is made for document access. It also specifies circumstances where notification is not required, such as when notifying would prejudice legal investigations, enforcement, confidential sources, safety, or national security. The onus of proof shifts to the agency or Minister when they apply for review, or to the affected third party when they are a party to the proceeding.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts data transfer impact assessments by requiring notification of affected third parties in certain cross-border data access reviews, with specific exemptions related to legal investigations, confidential sources, safety, and national security.

requirements.text: Agency or Minister must notify affected third parties as soon as practicable when a review application is made for document access
Notification is not required if notifying would prejudice legal investigations, enforcement, confidential sources, safety, or national security
Affected third party has onus of proof if they are a party to the proceeding

sections.source_quote: 60AA (2) The agency to which, or the Minister to whom, the request was made for access to the document must, as soon as practicable, take all reasonable steps to notify the affected third party for the document of the application to the Tribunal. (2) An agency or Minister is not required to notify an affected third party for the document under subsection 60AA(2) if: (a) the agency or the Minister applies to the Tribunal for an order that it would not be appropriate to notify the affected third party in the circumstances covered by subsection (3); and (b) the Tribunal makes the order. (3) The circumstances covered by this subsection are whether notifying the affected third party would, or could reasonably be expected to, do any of the following: (a) prejudice the conduct of an investigation of a breach, or possible breach, of the law, or a failure, or possible failure, to comply with a law relating to taxation; (b) prejudice the enforcement or proper administration of the law in a particular instance; (c) disclose, or enable a person to ascertain, the existence or identity of a confidential source of information, or the non-existence of a confidential source of information, in relation to the enforcement or administration of the law; (d) endanger the life or physical safety of any person; (e) cause damage to the security, defence or international relations of the Commonwealth.

sections.heading: Schedules 4 to (cont.)
sections.title: Information Commissioner investigations and complaints procedures
sections.summary: This section establishes procedures for the Information Commissioner to investigate complaints about agency actions under the Freedom of Information Act. It requires complaints to be in writing and identify the agency, allows the Information Commissioner to conduct preliminary inquiries, and provides discretion to not investigate complaints if the action is not within the agency's functions, the complainant has rights to review or complain elsewhere, or the complaint is frivolous. It also outlines procedures for transferring complaints to the Ombudsman when appropriate.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by defining procedures for investigating complaints about data access decisions, including when to transfer complaints to the Ombudsman for cross-border or multi-jurisdictional matters.

requirements.text: Complaints must be in writing and identify the agency
Information Commissioner may investigate complaints about agency actions
Information Commissioner may decide not to investigate if the action is not within agency functions
Information Commissioner may decide not to investigate if the complainant has rights to review or complain elsewhere
Information Commissioner may transfer complaints to Ombudsman if more effectively handled there

sections.source_quote: 70 (1) A person (the complainant) may complain to the Information Commissioner about an action taken by an agency in the performance of functions, or the exercise of powers, under this Act. (2) A complaint must: (a) be in writing; and (b) identify the agency (also the respondent agency) in respect of which the complaint is made. (3) The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner must provide appropriate assistance to a person who: (a) wishes to make a complaint; and (b) requires assistance to formulate the complaint. 74 (1) This section applies if the Information Commissioner is satisfied that a complaint about an action could be more effectively or appropriately dealt with by the Ombudsman under the Ombudsman Act 1976. (2) The Information Commissioner: (a) must consult the Ombudsman about the complaint with a view to avoiding inquiries being conducted into that matter by both the Information Commissioner and the Ombudsman; and (b) may decide not to investigate the action, or not to continue to investigate the action.

sections.heading: Note:
sections.title: Notice requirements for Information Commissioner investigations
sections.summary: Before starting an investigation, the Information Commissioner must notify the respondent agency in writing. If the Commissioner decides not to investigate or stop an investigation, they must notify the complainant and respondent agency within 14 days, including reasons for the decision. This ensures transparency and accountability in the investigation process.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires written notification to respondent agency before investigation start and decision on non-investigation must be communicated with reasons. Relevant for DTIA as it sets procedural safeguards for investigations.

requirements.text: Before beginning an investigation, the Information Commissioner must notify the respondent agency in writing.
If the Information Commissioner decides not to investigate or not to continue an investigation, they must notify the complainant and respondent agency within 14 days with reasons.

sections.source_quote: Before beginning an investigation, the Information Commissioner must notify the respondent agency in writing. Notice of decision not to investigate, or not to continue to investigate must be notified to complainant and respondent agency within 14 days with reasons.

sections.heading: Note: (cont.)
sections.title: Complaints to Ombudsman: Reporting Restrictions
sections.summary: This section requires that reports to the Ombudsman under the Ombudsman Act 1976 must exclude exempt matters and information specified in subsection 25(1) of the Ombudsman Act. The restriction applies to reports related to actions taken under the Freedom of Information Act 1982. Complaints must be made in good faith to trigger these obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Reports involving cross-border data transfers must exclude exempt information to prevent disclosure of sensitive data. This affects data sharing with third parties and international data flows.

requirements.text: A report under subsection 12(3) or section 15 or 17 of the Ombudsman Act 1976 in relation to an action taken under this Act must not include exempt matter
A report under subsection 12(3) or section 15 or 17 of the Ombudsman Act 1976 in relation to an action taken under this Act must not include information of the kind referred to in subsection 25(1)

sections.source_quote: A report under subsection 12(3) or section 15 or 17 of the Ombudsman Act 1976 in relation to an action taken under this Act must not include: (a) exempt matter; or (b) information of the kind referred to in subsection 25(1).

sections.heading: Note: (cont.)
sections.title: Vexatious Applicants: Declaration and Restrictions
sections.summary: This section allows the Information Commissioner to declare a person a vexatious applicant if they repeatedly engage in access actions with abuse of process, manifestly unreasonable actions, or specific abusive behaviors. The declaration may restrict the person from making requests, amendments to records, internal reviews, or IC reviews without written permission from the Information Commissioner.
sections.dtia_summary: Vexatious applicants cannot access sensitive data without authorization, impacting data sharing and cross-border transfers. This affects data subject rights and third-party processing.

requirements.text: The Information Commissioner may declare a person a vexatious applicant if they repeatedly engage in access actions involving abuse of the process
The Information Commissioner may declare a person a vexatious applicant if a particular access action involves abuse of the process
The Information Commissioner may declare a person a vexatious applicant if a particular access action would be manifestly unreasonable
A vexatious applicant declaration may restrict an agency or Minister from considering requests without written permission from the Information Commissioner
A vexatious applicant declaration may restrict the Information Commissioner from considering IC review applications made by the person

sections.source_quote: The Information Commissioner may, by written instrument (a vexatious applicant declaration), declare a person to be a vexatious applicant.

sections.heading: Note: (cont.)
sections.title: Replaces Privacy Commissioner with Information Commissioner in multiple acts
sections.summary: This section lists amendments to various Australian laws that replace references to the 'Privacy Commissioner' with 'Information Commissioner' in specific sections or definitions. The changes apply across multiple acts including the Australian Citizenship Act, Australian Human Rights Commission Act, Crimes Act, and others. These amendments update the legal references to align with the establishment of the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner.
sections.dtia_summary: This section updates legal references to the Information Commissioner across multiple acts, which may affect data transfer mechanisms where the Commissioner's role is involved in privacy functions. However, it does not directly impose new transfer restrictions.

requirements.text: Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner in relation to matters that relate to the privacy functions (within the meaning of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010)'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner'
Repeal the definition of Privacy Commissioner
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner' (wherever occurring), substitute 'Information Commissioner'

Omit 'Privacy Commissioner' (first occurring), substitute 'Information Commissioner in relation to matters that relate to the privacy functions (within the meaning of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010)'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner' (last occurring), substitute 'Information Commissioner'

sections.source_quote: Amendments consequential on the establishment of the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner Schedule 5

sections.heading: 20  Subsection 85ZZ(2)
sections.title: Replaces Privacy Commissioner with Information Commissioner in Freedom of Information Act
sections.summary: This section lists amendments to the Freedom of Information Act 1982 that replace references to the 'Privacy Commissioner' with 'Information Commissioner' in specific sections and definitions. The changes include updating definitions, headings, and sections to align with the new Information Commissioner role, ensuring consistency across Commonwealth contracts and agency documents.
sections.dtia_summary: These amendments update legal references to the Information Commissioner in the Freedom of Information Act, which may impact data handling and disclosure practices but do not directly affect cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner's', substitute 'Information Commissioner's'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner in relation to matters that relate to the privacy functions (within the meaning of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010)'
Repeal the section, substitute: Guidelines relating to privacy

sections.source_quote: Amendments consequential on the establishment of the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner Schedule 5

sections.heading: ComLaw Authoritative Act C2012C00866
sections.title: Replaces Privacy Commissioner with Information Commissioner in Freedom of Information Act
sections.summary: This section lists amendments to the Freedom of Information Act 1982 that replace references to the 'Privacy Commissioner' with 'Information Commissioner' in specific sections and definitions. The changes include updating definitions, headings, and sections to align with the new Information Commissioner role, ensuring consistency across Commonwealth contracts and agency documents.
sections.dtia_summary: These amendments update legal references to the Information Commissioner in the Freedom of Information Act, which may impact data handling and disclosure practices but do not directly affect cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner's', substitute 'Information Commissioner's'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner in relation to matters that relate to the privacy functions (within the meaning of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010)'
Repeal the section, substitute: Guidelines relating to privacy

sections.source_quote: Amendments consequential on the establishment of the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner Schedule 5

sections.heading: 42  Subsection 135AA(4)
sections.title: Replaces Privacy Commissioner with Information Commissioner in Freedom of Information Act
sections.summary: This section lists amendments to the Freedom of Information Act 1982 that replace references to the 'Privacy Commissioner' with 'Information Commissioner' in specific sections and definitions. The changes include updating definitions, headings, and sections to align with the new Information Commissioner role, ensuring consistency across Commonwealth contracts and agency documents.
sections.dtia_summary: These amendments update legal references to the Information Commissioner in the Freedom of Information Act, which may impact data handling and disclosure practices but do not directly affect cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner's', substitute 'Information Commissioner's'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner in relation to matters that relate to the privacy functions (within the meaning of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010)'
Repeal the section, substitute: Guidelines relating to privacy

sections.source_quote: Amendments consequential on the establishment of the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner Schedule 5

sections.heading: 42  Subsection 135AA(4) (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions for intelligence and defense intelligence documents
sections.summary: This section defines exemptions for intelligence and defense intelligence documents under the Freedom of Information Act 1982. It specifies that agencies and ministers are exempt from the Act's requirements for documents originating from specific intelligence agencies, defense intelligence organizations, or related materials. The exemptions cover both the original documents and summaries/extracts of these documents, with clear definitions of intelligence agency documents and defense intelligence documents.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes exemptions for certain sensitive government documents, which may impact data transfer assessments when handling intelligence or defense-related information across borders. The exemptions apply to specific document types and require careful assessment to avoid unintended disclosure of classified information.

requirements.text: An agency is exempt from the operation of this Act in relation to a document that has originated with, or has been received from, any of the specified intelligence agencies
A document containing a summary of, or an extract from, an intelligence agency document is exempt to the extent it contains such summary or extract
A Minister is exempt from the operation of this Act in relation to intelligence agency documents
A document containing a summary of, or an extract from, an intelligence agency document is exempt to the extent it contains such summary or extract
An agency is exempt from the operation of this Act in relation to defense intelligence documents
A document containing a summary of, or an extract from, a defense intelligence document is exempt to the extent it contains such summary or extract

sections.source_quote: An agency is exempt from the operation of this Act in relation to a document (an intelligence agency document) that has originated with, or has been received from, any of the following: (i) the Australian Secret Intelligence Service; (ii) the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation; (iii) the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security; (iv) the Office of National Assessments; (v) the Defence Imagery and Geospatial Organisation; (vi) the Defence Intelligence Organisation; (vii) the Defence Signals Directorate

sections.heading: 42  Subsection 135AA(4) (cont.)
sections.title: Request consultation process for practical refusal reasons
sections.summary: This section establishes a request consultation process that must be undertaken when an agency or minister determines that a practical refusal reason exists for a document request. The process requires written notification to the applicant, identification of the practical refusal reason, and a 14-day consultation period during which the applicant can revise their request. The process aims to allow applicants to modify their requests to overcome practical refusal reasons before a final decision is made.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines a specific consultation process that must be followed when handling requests involving sensitive or non-existent documents, which may impact data transfer assessments when dealing with government records across borders. The process ensures applicants have a chance to revise requests before final refusal decisions are made.

requirements.text: The agency or Minister must give the applicant written notice stating intention to refuse access, practical refusal reason, contact person details, and consultation period duration
The consultation period is 14 days after the applicant receives the notice
The agency or Minister must take reasonable steps to assist the applicant in revising the request to eliminate the practical refusal reason
The applicant must withdraw the request, make a revised request, or indicate they do not wish to revise the request within the consultation period

sections.source_quote: The agency or Minister must give the applicant a written notice stating: (a) an intention to refuse access to a document in accordance with a request; (b) the practical refusal reason; (c) the name of an officer of the agency or member of staff of the Minister (the contact person) with whom the applicant may consult during a period; (d) details of how the applicant may contact the contact person; (e) that the period (the consultation period) during which the applicant may consult with the contact person is 14 days after the day the applicant is given the notice.

sections.heading: 42  Subsection 135AA(4) (cont.)
sections.title: Transition of Privacy Commissioner responsibilities to Information Commissioner
sections.summary: This section amends the Freedom of Information Act 1982 by transitioning responsibilities from the Privacy Commissioner to the Information Commissioner. It ensures continuity of functions, including the handling of ongoing investigations, consultations, and decisions made before the commencement date. The Information Commissioner assumes all obligations and powers previously held by the Privacy Commissioner for matters that were initiated prior to the transition date.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that transitional obligations from the Privacy Commissioner to the Information Commissioner are maintained, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments involving cross-border data flows that may have been initiated before the transition date.

requirements.text: The Privacy Commissioner's actions before the commencement day are treated as actions by the Information Commissioner for the same purpose.
The Information Commissioner may determine exceptions to the transition for specific actions.
Consultations initiated by the Privacy Commissioner before the commencement day may continue with the Information Commissioner.
Decisions made by the Privacy Commissioner before the commencement day may be implemented by the Information Commissioner.
The Information Commissioner must handle submissions and evidence as required by the Privacy Commissioner before the transition date.

sections.source_quote: This item applies if: (a) the Privacy Commissioner made a decision in accordance with a provision of the Privacy Act 1988; and (b) having made the decision, the Privacy Commissioner was required to take a step to implement it; and (c) the Privacy Commissioner did not take the step before the commencement day. The Information Commissioner may take the step in accordance with the provision to implement the decision on or after the commencement day.

sections.heading: 42  Subsection 135AA(4) (cont.)
sections.title: Transition of Privacy Commissioner's pending proceedings to Information Commissioner
sections.summary: This section mandates that any pending court or tribunal proceedings where the Privacy Commissioner was a party before the commencement date automatically substitute the Information Commissioner as the party from the commencement date onward. It also outlines specific scenarios where the Information Commissioner may continue or complete actions initiated by the Privacy Commissioner under the Privacy Act 1988, such as reviews, examinations, conciliation processes, and conferences, without interruption to the legal process.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures continuity of legal proceedings involving the Privacy Commissioner by transitioning responsibilities to the Information Commissioner, which is relevant for DTIAs when assessing the impact of regulatory transitions on cross-border data handling in legal contexts.

requirements.text: The Information Commissioner substitutes for the Privacy Commissioner as a party to pending proceedings on and after the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may complete reviews, examinations, conciliation processes, research, or record examinations initiated by the Privacy Commissioner before the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may hold conferences convened by the Privacy Commissioner before the commencement day but not held.
The Information Commissioner may make determinations in relation to matters raised at conferences held by the Privacy Commissioner before the commencement day.

sections.source_quote: If any proceedings to which the Privacy Commissioner was a party were pending in any court or tribunal immediately before the commencement day, the Information Commissioner is substituted for the Privacy Commissioner as a party to the proceedings on and after the commencement day.

sections.heading: Schedule 2,
sections.title: Commencement date for Schedule 2 item 16
sections.summary: This section specifies that Schedule 2, item 16 commences immediately after the commencement date of item 23 in Schedule 3 of the Freedom of Information Amendment (Reform) Act 2010, which is set for 1 November 2010.

requirements.text: Schedule 2, item 16 commences immediately after the time specified in the Freedom of Information Amendment (Reform) Act 2010 for the commencement of item 23 of Schedule 3 to that Act.

sections.source_quote: 16. Schedule 2, item 16 Immediately after the time specified in the Freedom of Information Amendment (Reform) Act 2010 for the commencement of item 23 of Schedule 3 to that Act. 1 November 2010

sections.heading: Schedule 2,
sections.title: Commencement date for Schedule 2 items 17 and 18
sections.summary: This section specifies that Schedule 2, items 17 and 18 commence immediately after the commencement date of Schedule 4 to the Freedom of Information Amendment (Reform) Act 2010, which is set for 1 November 2010.

requirements.text: Schedule 2, items 17 and 18 commence immediately after the time specified in the Freedom of Information Amendment (Reform) Act 2010 for the commencement of Schedule 4 to that Act.

sections.source_quote: 17. Schedule 2, items 17 and 18 Immediately after the time specified in the Freedom of Information Amendment (Reform) Act 2010 for the commencement of Schedule 4 to that Act. 1 November 2010
